upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a surface temp of 11.6°C with a moist dewpoint spread of only 4.7°C at the surface, indicating residual marine moisture in the lower levels. The 850 mb level (1477 m) sits at 2.4°C with a near-saturated spread of 1.6°C, suggesting a shallow moist layer capping thermal development; the surface-to-850mb lapse rate of 6.24°C/km is near moist adiabatic and slightly sub-dry-adiabatic, meaning thermals will be modest and capped relatively low. Winds increase substantially with height: 25 kts from 250° at 850mb, 39 kts at 700mb from 285°, and 49 kts at 500mb from 290°, indicating strong upper-level westerly flow well above ridge height that does not directly impact low-level flying but signals a progressive, somewhat active airmass.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area/SF region. METAR notes: KWVI (Watsonville) reporting OVC050 with earlier OVC011 and recent rain (RAE1157), indicating marine/low-cloud influence pushing north from the south bay — watch for afternoon cloud encroachment. KHAF (Half Moon Bay) cleared from BKN046 to CLR by 1435Z, dew point at saturation (08/08) flagging a very shallow marine layer that recently mixed out. KAPC (Napa) calm and clear, confirming inland heating underway. KRHV (Reid-Hillview) SCT020 at 1358Z with light N winds — some residual low cloud near Ed Levin launch area early.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas foothills — NNE-facing high launch, inland thermal/ridge hybrid site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's upper launch works best with NW-to-N surface winds 8–15 kts driving ridge lift along the west-facing escarpment, combined with thermal development from the heated Santa Clara Valley floor below. The sea breeze typically fills in from the NW by midday, extending flyable conditions into late afternoon.
sites.todaySetup: Today's upper launch looks like a solid flyable day, with consistent NNW winds and a building boundary layer driving the call. HRRR and GFS are in full agreement: surface winds at 1750 ft hold 10–13 kts from 340–023° throughout the day, right in the sweet spot for this site. The boundary layer climbs from ~370 m at 0800 to a peak of ~920 m by 1400 local, giving thermals enough ceiling to work with but nothing dramatic — expect moderate, organized lift rather than rowdy conditions. Tomorrow.io gusts peak at 15–18 kts in the 1500–1600 window, which is manageable but worth noting for less experienced pilots. No precip, near-zero cloud cover through the afternoon, and zero CAPE keep the air honest. The one caveat is the sub-dry-adiabatic lapse rate from the sounding, which means thermals will be real but not punchy — a calm, rewarding soaring day rather than a XC bomber.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNW 10–11 kts at launch, BL only 370–725 m. KRHV showing SCT020 early so some residual low cloud possible over the valley. Soarable on ridge lift but thermals just beginning to fire.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: winds 9–11 kts from 344–012°, BL climbing to 830–1045 m. Clean, organized conditions with minimal cloud. Thermals moderate and well-structured — ideal for P2/P3.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds tick up to 11–13 kts from 020–023° and gusts reach 15–18 kts per Tomorrow.io around 1500–1600. BL holds 860–920 m before tapering. Still flyable but conditions become slightly more demanding; P2 pilots should land by 1500.

verdict.hazards: Afternoon gusts to 18 kts 1500–1600; sub-adiabatic lapse rate keeps thermals moderate — no large surprises but some punch possible

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas foothills — lower training hill launch, valley-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is a training/low-airtime site best suited to gentle NW-to-N breezes of 8–12 kts. It is thermally driven from the valley floor but requires benign, consistent wind without punchy gusts. The lower site is more sheltered but also more susceptible to cycle gaps and rotor from surrounding terrain.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft site looks workable but tighter than the upper launch today. Winds are marginally lighter at the lower elevation — 8–11 kts NNW — but the key concern is the Tomorrow.io gust signature reaching 14–17 kts in the 1500–1700 window, which is on the upper edge for a training hill. The boundary layer grows to ~1045 m by 1400, which is healthy, but from a lower starting point thermal triggers will take longer to reach the launch. KRHV's SCT020 early in the morning adds some uncertainty about cloud shadow dampening early thermals. Conditions are usable in the 1100–1400 window for P2 pilots with their instructor; independent P2 flying in the afternoon gust cycles is not recommended. GFS and HRRR agree well here.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NNW 9–11 kts, BL 385–830 m. Some residual low cloud possible (KRHV SCT020). Soarable for confident P2 pilots but thermals weak and inconsistent early.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window for the lower site: winds 8–9 kts from 340–014°, BL 985–1045 m, minimal cloud. Thermals moderate and organized. P2-friendly with supervision.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds increase to 10–12 kts and Tomorrow.io gusts hit 14–17 kts by 1600. Cycle gaps and brief punchy thermals make this marginal for P2 solo. P3 pilots comfortable.

verdict.hazards: Afternoon gusts to 17 kts; training site — punchy cycles after 1400 exceed P2 solo limits

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site powered by the NW sea breeze. It needs consistent W-to-NW surface winds of 10–20 kts to generate reliable ridge lift along the coastal bluff face. Too light and you sink out; too strong and rotor below the bluff becomes dangerous. Marine layer burn-off timing is critical — fog or low stratus on the face kills the site.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock looks flyable today with solid NW ridge winds and good visibility. The deciding factors are the clean NNW flow and the marine layer already burning off — KHAF went from BKN046 at 1255Z to CLR by 1355–1435Z, confirming the coastal marine layer thinned out by mid-morning. SF Bar buoy (46026) is reporting 13.6 kts from 340° with SST 12.8°C; Half Moon Bay buoy showing 13.6 kts from 340° with SST 13.4°C. The relatively mild SST (not cold) supports a shallower, faster-clearing marine layer — consistent with the KHAF burn-off observed. HRRR and GFS fully agree: NNW winds 9.6–12.1 kts at the surface, ramping to 12–16 kts at 80 m elevation, through the entire afternoon. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 16–18.5 kts in the 1500–1600 window — slightly punchy but within the site's operating envelope for P2/P3. No precip, zero CAPE, cloud-free afternoon. A very usable ridge soaring day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: KHAF cleared by ~1355Z so the face should be soarable from 0930–1000 onward. Winds 7–10 kts NNW building — light early but sufficient for experienced pilots to get established on the ridge.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 9.6–11 kts NNW, BL 875–1025 m above sea level, minimal cloud. Textbook Mussel Rock ridge soaring. P2 pilots welcome in this window.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds hold 11–12 kts NNW with gusts to 16–18.5 kts per Tomorrow.io around 1500–1600. Still ridgeable but slightly more active; P2 pilots should be heads-up for gust cycles off the bluff edge.

verdict.hazards: Afternoon gusts to 18.5 kts; residual rotor possible on bluff edge during gust pulses; KHAF dew point at saturation suggests fog can return quickly after sunset

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont hills — NW-facing ridge/thermal hybrid, East Bay
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on NW sea breeze combined with thermal development from the hot Fremont/Tri-Valley floor. Ideal winds are 8–15 kts from the NW-to-N. It is a technical site with significant rotor potential in the lee of the ridge when winds are strong or gusty, and thermals can be punchy in the afternoon.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak is flyable today but the window is narrower and more pilot-dependent than Ed Levin. The core issue is wind direction and gust behavior: GFS/HRRR show 10–12 kts from 340–023° at ridge height, which is on-axis, but Tomorrow.io paints a more variable picture — light and variable (2–4 kts) at 1400–1500 with gusts only to 8–12 kts, then ramping to 7–9 kts by 1600–1900 with gusts 11–15 kts. This model spread suggests the sea breeze front arrives at the peak later or is less organized than at Ed Levin. The boundary layer climbs to 920 m by 1400 which supports moderate thermals, but the moist lapse rate from the sounding means thermals will be moderate rather than strong. KAPC shows calm, clear conditions confirming inland heating is underway. The afternoon gust cycles are manageable for P3 pilots but the variable early-afternoon winds make timing critical. P2 pilots should treat this as marginal and wait for confirmed steady flow before launching.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: GFS/HRRR show 10–11 kts NNW; Tomorrow.io is lighter at 2–5 kts suggesting delayed sea breeze arrival. BL only 370–725 m. Uncertain and not recommended for solo P2 — model disagreement is a red flag.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: HRRR winds 9.6–10.9 kts from 354–012°, BL 830–920 m. If the sea breeze fills in as GFS/HRRR suggest, this is the best window — moderate thermals and on-axis flow. Confirm conditions on launch before committing.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds increase to 11–12.6 kts from 020–023° per HRRR; Tomorrow.io gusts 11–15 kts. Thermals more active and potentially punchy as BL peaks at 920 m. P3 comfortable, P2 should be on the ground by 1500.

verdict.bestWindow: 11:30 AM – 2:30 PM (if sea breeze fills in)
verdict.wind: 9–12 kts NNW/N (model spread — verify on launch)
verdict.hazards: Model disagreement on AM wind speed; rotor risk in lee if winds exceed 14 kts; afternoon gust cycles to 15 kts; technical site

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow (Friday Apr 3) looks significantly windier and borderline. HRRR/GFS both show 15–17 kts from 039–045° at 0800–1000 — a NE-ish component that is off-axis for this site. Tomorrow.io gusts reach 24–30 kts by afternoon. BL tops out at only 345–480 m suggesting strong subsidence capping. Expect turbulent, cross-wind conditions. MARGINAL to NO FLY depending on exact wind direction at launch.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Friday looks rough for the lower site — 20–23 kts from NE at the 600 ft elevation per HRRR, with gusts to 32 kts per Tomorrow.io by 1600. Off-axis NE flow and high gusts. NO FLY for the training hill.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Friday turns problematic at Mussel Rock — HRRR shows winds shifting to E/ENE (073–101°) in the morning, completely off-axis for the NW-facing bluff, with high cloud cover. Tomorrow.io holds a NW direction but is in strong disagreement with HRRR. With the NE/E flow risk and strong gusts (up to 30 kts per Tomorrow.io afternoon), this is likely MARGINAL to NO FLY — monitor closely Friday AM.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Friday shows 15–17 kts from 039–045° — off-axis NE component — with gusts to 29 kts per Tomorrow.io afternoon. Low BL (170–480 m) and strong winds make this a NO FLY day for Mission Peak.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday Apr 4 shows a dramatic wind calm-down — GFS drops to 2–6 kts with variable direction. BL climbs to 1265–1605 m by afternoon, the best thermal ceiling of the 4-day period. If morning winds establish a light NW component, this could be an excellent thermal XC day. Monitor closely — light wind days can produce strong unorganized thermals.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday looks calm and potentially thermally productive with BL to 1265 m. Very light winds (2–6 kts) mean the site may be glassy and thermal-only. Good for experienced pilots hunting thermals; uncertain for P2 who need consistent ridge wind.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Saturday shows very light winds (4–5 kts from variable/ENE directions) — not enough for reliable ridge lift. BL climbs to 885–1385 m but the coastal marine layer may return with high cloud cover (99% by late afternoon). Not a ridge day; thermal-only possibility but marginal at best.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Saturday looks like the best thermal day of the week — BL to 1195–1285 m, light 5–6 kts N-to-NNW winds. Low CAPE and no precip. Could produce good organized thermals in the midday window. Worth watching.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available beyond Apr 4 in this dataset. Trend from the 4-day window suggests a gradual return of NW flow after the weekend calm. Monitor extended forecasts.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same as upper launch — day 3 data not available. Pattern suggests continued light/variable with possible NW return.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available. If the light/variable pattern persists from Saturday, Sunday may remain marginal for ridge soaring. Marine layer recovery possible.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 data not available. If Saturday's light thermal day pattern holds, Sunday could offer another thermal-focused window. Watch for sea breeze filling in.

watchlist: KWVI (Watsonville) reported OVC011 and recent rain this morning — watch for marine/low-cloud layer pushing north along the coast and south bay through the afternoon
Tomorrow.io and HRRR show significant disagreement on Mission Peak morning winds (2–4 kts vs 10–11 kts) — verify wind at launch before flying
Friday Apr 3 looks significantly windier with NE-component flow at all sites — likely a NO FLY or MARGINAL day across the board; plan accordingly
Saturday Apr 4 may offer excellent thermal conditions with BL to 1600 m at Ed Levin — best XC potential of the 4-day window but light winds require good thermal reading skills
KHAF dew point at saturation (08/08) despite cleared skies — fog can reform quickly at Mussel Rock after sunset; land well before dark
tldr: Today is a solid flying day — Mussel Rock and Ed Levin 1750 ft are the clear picks, with clean NNW 10–12 kt winds and a usable thermal ceiling developing by late morning. Best window is 11 AM to 3 PM across both sites. Skip Friday (strong off-axis winds incoming) and save your energy for Saturday's potential thermal bonanza.
